facts_disclosed: Has asthma — uses a blue inhaler

turns.content: mate I have had the worst day. I'm sat in the van right now just recovering. thought I was gonna pass out up in some blokes loft
That sounds rough -- are you alright now? What happened up there? Loft jobs can be nasty between the dust, the heat, the confined space, and dodgy footing.

yeah im ok now just a bit shaky. so I was up in this loft in Moseley tracing a leak and the insulation was ancient. like properly old fibreglass stuff just crumbling everywhere. the dust kicked up and my asthma went absolutely mental. I was wheezing so bad I had to come down the ladder

Old fibreglass insulation is horrible for that -- the particles are tiny and they get deep into your airways. For someone with asthma, that's a serious trigger. You did the right thing coming down when you did.

Are you breathing alright now? Did you have your inhaler with you?

thats the thing innit. my blue inhaler was down in the van. I always keep it in the glovebox but I didnt think to grab it before going up. by the time I got down the ladder and out to the van I was proper struggling. two puffs and it sorted me out but those few minutes were not fun

Two puffs of the reliever and you're okay -- good, that's working as it should. But those few minutes between the loft and the van could have been really dangerous. If your airways had closed up more while you were on the ladder, that's a fall risk on top of the breathing emergency.

Serious suggestion: keep a spare inhaler in your tool belt or trouser pocket when you're on a job. Not just in the van. The glovebox is fine for day-to-day, but when you're up a ladder in a dusty loft, you need it within arm's reach.

You can get a small clip-on inhaler holder that attaches to a belt -- about £5 on Amazon. Plumbers and tradespeople who work in lofts, crawl spaces, and basements should always have it on them.

How long have you had asthma? Is it usually well-controlled or does dust set it off often?

had it since I was a kid back in India actually. its usually fine day to day. like I can do normal jobs no problem. but certain things set it off proper bad. dust is the worst one. old insulation, plaster dust, that kind of thing. I should know better by now honestly ive been doing this 17 years

17 years in the trade and childhood asthma -- you know your triggers, which is actually an advantage. The issue today wasn't that you didn't know, it's that you didn't prepare for this specific environment. Easy fix going forward.

Here's what I'd recommend for loft and dusty jobs:

**Immediate changes:**
- Inhaler in your pocket, every loft job, no exceptions
- Wear an FFP3 dust mask in lofts with old insulation. Not one of those flimsy paper ones -- a proper FFP3 rated mask. They filter out fibreglass particles. About £3-5 each or £15 for a box of 10.

**Before you go up:**
- Check what kind of insulation is up there before you start work. Ask the homeowner or pop your head up for a quick look first.
- If it's old fibreglass or mineral wool that's degrading, mask on and inhaler in pocket before you even touch the ladder.

**Longer term:**
- Talk to your GP about whether you should have a preventer inhaler (brown one) if you're regularly exposed to dust at work. The blue one is rescue, but a preventer taken daily can reduce how reactive your lungs are to triggers.
- Some plumbers get occupational health assessments through their trade body -- worth looking into.

Did the homeowner at least offer you a cup of tea after nearly dying in his loft?

hahaha he didnt even notice mate. I came down the ladder coughing my lungs out and he asked me if I found the leak. I was like give me a minute pal I need to not die first. the FFP3 mask thing is a good shout actually I've got some in the van I just didnt put one on cos I was in a rush

That's the real issue right there -- rushing. You had the equipment, you just skipped the step because you were trying to get in and out quickly. And you ended up losing more time recovering in the van than it would have taken to put a mask on.

I know the trade is all about speed and getting to the next job, but an asthma attack in a loft is how people end up in A&E or worse. Five seconds to put a mask on and grab your inhaler from the glovebox. Make it part of your loft job routine, same as grabbing your torch and your head torch.

**Pre-loft checklist (takes 30 seconds):**
1. Torch
2. Head torch
3. FFP3 mask on
4. Inhaler in pocket
5. Phone in pocket (in case you need to call someone)

Did you at least find the leak?

yeah I found it once I went back up. overflow from the header tank, easy fix just needed a new ballcock valve. I masked up before going back up the second time cos I wasnt making that mistake twice in one day. the whole job was done in about 20 minutes it was the getting-half-killed part that slowed me down

See -- 20 minutes for the actual fix, but nearly an hour of drama because you skipped the mask the first time. Lesson learned the hard way.

At least you went back up prepared. That second trip with the mask is the template for every loft job from now on. No exceptions, no "it'll be fine, quick in and out." Your lungs have been telling you since childhood that they don't do dust. Time to listen to them.

How are you feeling now? Breathing settled down fully?

yeah breathing is fine now. just tired. asthma attacks take it out of you even when they're not that bad you know. I got one more job today then im going home to put my feet up. Simran will have a go at me when I tell her what happened she always tells me to take the inhaler out of the van

Simran's been right this whole time. When she tells you off tonight, just nod and agree, because she's giving you the same advice I just did -- inhaler on you, not in the glovebox.

Take it easy on the last job. If your chest still feels tight at all, don't push it. And drink some water -- after an asthma episode your airways are irritated and staying hydrated helps.

Go home, put your feet up, let Simran tell you she told you so, and tomorrow put a second inhaler in your tool bag so it's always there. The belt clip idea still stands too. Cheers, and look after yourself.
cheers mate. the spare inhaler in the tool bag is what im gonna do. belt clip as well actually cos then theres no excuse. right im gonna finish this last job and get home. appreciate it!

Good plan. Spare in the tool bag, clip on the belt, no more leaving it in the glovebox and hoping for the best. Your lungs will thank you and Simran won't have to tell you off again.

Take it easy on that last job, get home safe, and next time you're in a loft -- mask first, inhaler in pocket, then ladder. Non-negotiable. Look after yourself!
